name: Alto Rey Climb via Villares de Jadraque
name_original: Subida a El Picoz — ida y vuelta desde Hiendelaencina
description: Cycle the Alto Rey Climb via Villares de Jadraque, a difficult 25.0 miles (40.2 km) road cycling route with 3421 feet (1043 metres) of eleva
region_intro: Road cycling routes around Hiendelaencina traverse a region characterized by diverse geography, including demanding climbs and expansive plains. The landscape features a dramatic backdrop of mountains, lush forests, and green fields, with the Embalse de Alcorlo (Alcorlo Reservoir) being a prominent natural attraction. This area offers varied terrain suitable for road cyclists seeking both challenging ascents and smoother, high-speed sections. Best road cycling routes around Hiendelaencina The most popular road cycling route is Borderlands Loop from Pinilla de Jadraque, a moderate 38.7 miles (62.3 km) trail that takes 2 hours 52 minutes to complete. This route explores the borderlands region with varied terrain. Another top favourite among local road cyclists is Alto Rey Climb via Villares de Jadraque, a difficult 25.0 miles (40.2 km) path. It features significant elevation gain towards Alto Rey. Local road cyclists also love the View of Atienza Castle loop from Pálmaces de Jadraque, a 35.9 miles (57.8 km) trail leading through scenic landscapes with views of Atienza Castle, often completed in about 2 hours 31 minutes. Road cycling around Hiendelaencina is defined by mountains, reservoirs, and historic mining areas. The network offers options for moderate and difficult ability levels. The routes in Hiendelaencina are highly rated by the komoot community with an average score of 5.0 stars from 1 review. More than 100 road cyclists have used komoot to explore Hiendelaencina's varied terrain.
region_faq:
Q: How many road cycling routes are available around Hiendelaencina?
A: There are 10 road cycling routes around Hiendelaencina, offering a mix of moderate and difficult options for various skill levels.

Q: What kind of terrain can I expect when road cycling in Hiendelaencina?
A: Road cycling in Hiendelaencina offers a diverse landscape, from demanding climbs that test experienced cyclists to expansive plains ideal for high-speed rides. You'll traverse mountains, lush forests, and green fields, with prominent features like the Embalse de Alcorlo integrated into many routes.

Q: Are there challenging road cycling routes for experienced cyclists in the area?
A: Yes, Hiendelaencina offers 5 difficult road cycling routes. For instance, the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e1017172610/alto-rey-climb-via-villares-de-jadraque" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Alto Rey Climb via Villares de Jadraque&lt;/a&gt; features significant elevation gain, and the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/20142753" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;View of Atienza Castle – Hiendelaencina loop from Hiendelaencina&lt;/a&gt; is a longer, demanding option.

Q: What natural features can I see along the road cycling routes?
A: Many routes incorporate stunning natural features. You can expect to see the prominent &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/5855101" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Alcorlo Reservoir&lt;/a&gt;, and some routes offer views towards the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/4187789" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Alto Rey Peak&lt;/a&gt;. The region is characterized by mountains, forests, and green fields, providing a scenic backdrop for your ride.

Q: Are there any historical landmarks or points of interest along the cycling routes?
A: Hiendelaencina has a rich history, particularly as a silver mining center. While cycling, you might encounter remnants of this industrial past. Routes like the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/39560151" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;View of Atienza Castle loop from Pálmaces de Jadraque&lt;/a&gt; offer views of historic castles, and the village of &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/4681084" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Hiendelaencina&lt;/a&gt; itself is known for its unique 'black and golden architecture'.

Q: What is the best time of year for road cycling in Hiendelaencina?
A: The mild Mediterranean climate prevalent for much of the year makes Hiendelaencina an excellent destination for road cycling. Spring and autumn generally offer the most comfortable temperatures for longer rides, though summer can also be pleasant, especially in the mornings or late afternoons.

Q: Are there any circular road cycling routes available?
A: Yes, many of the road cycling routes around Hiendelaencina are circular. Examples include the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e1375734695/borderlands-loop-from-pinilla-de-jadraque" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Borderlands Loop from Pinilla de Jadraque&lt;/a&gt; and the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/20142371" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Galve de Sorbe Castle loop from Veguillas&lt;/a&gt;, allowing you to start and end in the same location.

Q: What do other road cyclists say about the routes in Hiendelaencina?
A: The road cycling routes in Hiendelaencina are highly rated by the komoot community, with an average score of 5.0 stars. Cyclists often praise the varied terrain, from challenging climbs to scenic plains, and the opportunity to explore the region's natural beauty and historical sites.

Q: Are there any easier road cycling routes for beginners or those seeking a more relaxed ride?
A: While Hiendelaencina is known for its challenging terrain, there are moderate routes available. The &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e1375734695/borderlands-loop-from-pinilla-de-jadraque" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Borderlands Loop from Pinilla de Jadraque&lt;/a&gt; is a moderate option that explores varied terrain without the extreme elevation gains of some other routes.

Q: Where can I find parking for road cycling routes in Hiendelaencina?
A: Many routes start from villages like Hiendelaencina, Pinilla de Jadraque, or Pálmaces de Jadraque. These villages typically offer public parking options, often near the town center or specific trailheads. It's advisable to check local parking availability upon arrival.

Q: What is the typical elevation gain on road cycling routes in this region?
A: The elevation gain varies significantly depending on the route. For example, the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e1017172610/alto-rey-climb-via-villares-de-jadraque" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Alto Rey Climb via Villares de Jadraque&lt;/a&gt; involves over 1000 meters of ascent, while the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/39560151" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;View of Atienza Castle loop from Pálmaces de Jadraque&lt;/a&gt; has around 565 meters of elevation gain, offering options for different fitness levels.
tips:
A village in the Sierra Norte of Guadalajara, at an altitude of approximately 1,084 meters.
Its origins are medieval, possibly predating the Bronze Age. The name derives from the Arabic Hisn al-Ancina (“castle of the holm oak”).
In 1844, the discovery of silver triggered a “silver rush,” transforming Hiendelaencina into a major 19th-century mining district, with up to 200 mines and a population that reached 5,000.
A parish church dedicated to Saint Cecilia was built between 1848 and 1851, financed with funds from the mining town.

Hard ascent that begins in the town of Bustares and where there are about six kilometers where at the end, it perfectly exceeds 10%. Without a doubt, one of the toughest ports in the province of Guadalajara!

Municipality that among its cultural heritage stands out the Romanesque church of San Lorenzo which, built in the 13th century, preserves the belfry and the Romanesque portal. The municipality also has the hermitage of Santo Alto Rey which, at 1,852 meters high, is of Templar origin.

faqs.question: How challenging is the Alto Rey Climb via Villares de Jadraque for road cyclists?
faqs.answer: This route is classified as a "difficult" racebike tour, requiring "very good fitness." It's considered one of the most demanding ascents in Castilla-La Mancha, earning it the nickname "El Rey" (The King) among climbs in the region. The final 8 kilometers are progressively steeper, with gradients exceeding 10% after the turn-off to Alto Rey, demanding considerable endurance.

faqs.question: What is the best time of year to cycle this route?
faqs.answer: While not explicitly stated, given the significant elevation gain and the region's climate, spring and autumn are generally the best seasons for cycling the Alto Rey Climb. Summers can be very hot, and winters may bring cold temperatures and potential snow at higher elevations, especially near the 1770-meter summit.

faqs.question: Are there any permits or entrance fees required to cycle in the Parque Natural de la Sierra Norte de Guadalajara?
faqs.answer: Generally, there are no specific permits or entrance fees required for road cycling on public roads within the Parque Natural de la Sierra Norte de Guadalajara. However, always respect local regulations, especially concerning protected areas and private land. The summit concludes at a military zone, so be aware of any restricted access in that immediate vicinity.

faqs.question: Where can I park to start the Alto Rey Climb?
faqs.answer: The initial phase of the climb begins near the bridge over the Bornova river, between Hiendelaencina and Villares de Jadraque. You can typically find parking within the villages of Hiendelaencina or Villares de Jadraque to begin your ride.

faqs.question: What kind of scenery can I expect along the way?
faqs.answer: The early sections of the climb are quite picturesque, featuring appealing landscapes and switchbacks, offering good photo opportunities. As you gain elevation, especially towards the summit, you'll be treated to broad views over the Guadalajara province. While some might find it less vibrant than northern Spanish landscapes, it's considered beautiful by La Mancha standards.

faqs.question: Is the Alto Rey Climb via Villares de Jadraque dog-friendly?
faqs.answer: This route is a road cycling climb, primarily on paved roads. While dogs are generally allowed in the surrounding natural park, bringing a dog on a challenging road cycling route like this is not recommended due to safety concerns for both the cyclist and the animal, especially with the sustained climbs and potential for traffic.

faqs.question: What are the key landmarks or points of interest on this cycling route?
faqs.answer: Along the route, you'll pass through the settlement of Hiendelaencina and near Bustares Church. The ultimate destination is El Picoz, Alto Rey Peak, which offers extensive views over the province of Guadalajara from its 1770-meter summit.

faqs.question: What is the road surface like on the Alto Rey Climb?
faqs.answer: The road surface along the Alto Rey Climb is generally in good condition, making it suitable for racebikes. Traffic is typically light, especially on the higher sections, allowing for a more focused and enjoyable climbing experience.

faqs.question: Does this route intersect with other well-known cycling or hiking paths?
faqs.answer: Yes, while primarily a dedicated road cycling climb, sections of this route overlap with other paths. Notably, about 85% of it follows the official cycling route known as [CIMA GU03] Alto Rey * Villares de Jadraque. Smaller portions also intersect with the Camino del Cid (Senderista) and Camino del Cid.

faqs.question: Are there any cafes or places to refuel along the route?
faqs.answer: The route passes through or near villages like Hiendelaencina and Bustares. These settlements may offer limited options for cafes or small shops to refuel, especially in Villares de Jadraque. However, it's always advisable to carry sufficient water and nutrition for this challenging and lengthy climb.

faqs.question: What makes the final section of the Alto Rey Climb particularly difficult?
faqs.answer: The final phase, spanning a little over 8 kilometers, is significantly more sustained and progressively steeper. Gradients increase from a manageable 2-4% near Bustares to 7-8% after the town, eventually exceeding 10% after the turn-off to Alto Rey. This sustained steepness at higher elevation is what truly tests a cyclist's endurance and willpower.
